text: A severe storm hit Sydney and surrounding areas, leaving over 250,000 homes and businesses without power and isolating 4,500 people. Destructive winds and flooding have caused four deaths and prompted over 13,000 calls for help. Schools and public transportation have been disrupted, and a local creek overflowed into a neighborhood, causing flooding.

A powerful storm has left 250,000 homes and businesses without power in Sydney and surrounding areas, with 4,500 people isolated by floodwaters. The storm brought destructive winds, flooding, and four deaths. Emergency services have received 13,000 calls for help and have conducted 144 rescues. Schools, colleges, and public transportation have been disrupted. The storm was caused by an intense low-pressure system, with wind gusts reaching 135 km/h (85 mph) and 200 mm (7.87 in) of rainfall in some areas.

Officer Michael Slager's five-year career with the North Charleston Police Department in South Carolina ended after he resorted to deadly force following a routine traffic stop. Slager, 33, has been fired, officials said Wednesday. His wife is eight months' pregnant and the city will continue paying for her medical insurance until the baby is born, North Charleston Mayor Keith Summey told reporters. Slager initially told investigators that he used a Taser in a confrontation Saturday with Walter Scott, who was pulled over for a busted taillight. But Scott went for the Taser, according to Slager. A widely circulated video of the shooting death of the 50-year-old unarmed African-American man by the white police officer tells another story: Scott attempts to run. His back is to Slager, who, from a few yards away, raises his gun and fires. Slager is now charged with murder. The FBI is involved in the investigation of the slaying of the father of four. "Shots fired and the subject is down," Slager said moments after the shooting, according to reports. "He took my Taser."

text: Officer Michael Slager has been fired from the North Charleston Police Department in South Carolina after he used deadly force during a routine traffic stop. Slager initially claimed that he used a Taser in a confrontation with Walter Scott, the man he pulled over for a broken taillight, but a video of the incident tells a different story. In the video, Scott can be seen attempting to run away before Slager shoots him in the back. Slager has now been charged with murder and the FBI is involved in the investigation. This is not the first complaint against Slager, as he was previously accused of using excessive force and tasing a man without reason. The man who filed the complaint plans to file a lawsuit against Slager. Slager had been with the police department for five years and was considered proficient with a Taser. He had passed all necessary certifications and training, including in firearms and Taser use. He had also completed courses on bias-based profiling and ethics. Slager's career history also includes a nonpreventable traffic accident and prior discussions with a supervisor about procedures for conducting motor vehicle stops. Walter Scott, the victim in this incident, was an unarmed African-American man and the father of four. The shooting has sparked outrage and condemnation from South Carolina senators and the governor.

Slager had been on the force for five years and had a history of misconduct, including allegations of using excessive force and filing false reports. text: Officer Michael Slager of the North Charleston Police Department in South Carolina has been fired and charged with murder after a video surfaced of him shooting an unarmed black man, Walter Scott, during a routine traffic stop. text: Ex-New England Patriot Aaron Hernandez has pleaded not guilty to orchestrating the murder of Odin Lloyd, a man who was dating the sister of his fiancee, Shayanna Jenkins. Hernandez is charged with multiple offenses including murder and two weapons charges. Prosecutors allege that Hernandez executed Lloyd in June 2013, and that evidence found at the scene led to Hernandez being charged with two more murders in Boston. Hernandez is also accused of shooting Alexander Bradley in the face in February 2013, four months before Lloyd's death. Bradley testified against Hernandez in the Lloyd case, and is suing Hernandez for the alleged shooting. Hernandez's fiancee, Shayanna Jenkins, is also implicated in the case, and is accused of perjury for lying to a grand jury about guns in their home. The trial has involved a complex cast of characters including Hernandez's fiancee, her sister, who was dating the victim, and Hernandez's cousin, in whose garage police found the SUV linked to the Boston double homicide.

text:
Former New England Patriot, Aaron Hernandez, is currently on trial for the murder of Odin Lloyd, who was dating the sister of Hernandez’s fiancée. Hernandez has pleaded not guilty to murder and two weapons charges. The trial began in January and the jury began deliberations on Tuesday. The prosecutor, William McCauley, called Hernandez the trigger man during closing arguments. Although the motive for the murder is still unclear, it is not legally required to secure a conviction. However, jurors generally prefer to know the motive. The evidence collected in Lloyd’s death has also led to two more murder charges against Hernandez in a separate case in Boston. This trial is scheduled to begin in May but may be pushed back. The trial has involved several key individuals, including two sisters who played important roles in the lives of both Hernandez and Lloyd.

Odin Lloyd was a 27-year-old semi-pro football player who was found dead less than a mile from Hernandez’s home in North Attleboro, Massachusetts. He had been shot six times. Daniel de Abreu and Safiro Furtado were also killed in a separate incident outside a Boston nightclub. Hernandez is alleged to have been involved in their murders as well. Alexander Bradley, Hernandez’s former right-hand man, claims that Hernandez shot him in the face in February 2013 and is now suing him. Carlos Ortiz and Ernest Wallace are also charged with murder in Lloyd’s death. Hernandez’s fiancée, Shayanna Jenkins, is charged with perjury and her sister, Shaneah Jenkins, was dating Lloyd at the time of his death. Other individuals involved in the case include Tanya Cummings-Singleton, Thaddeus Singleton, Shaquilla Thibou, Terri Hernandez, Dennis Hernandez, and DJ Hernandez.

The prosecution team leading the case against Hernandez includes District Attorney Samuel Sutter, Assistant District Attorneys William McCauley and Patrick Bomberg. The defense team consists of attorneys James Sultan, Michael Fee, and Charles Rankin, who are confident that Hernandez will be exonerated.
